cover.title: THE SOIL
cover.subtitle: The one who makes every room easier, then forgets to stand somewhere too.
cover.tagline: You connect everyone else, but your own place keeps disappearing in the middle.

profile_scores.caption: High warmth, low pride — you adjust for everyone, but never quite make a place for yourself.

short_summary: You have a gift for making people feel connected, comfortable, and understood before they even ask.
But because you smooth everything over so quickly, almost no one notices when you're uncomfortable too.
What changes love for you is learning not to mediate every conflict before others learn how to meet you directly.

full_report.headline: THE SOIL
full_report.intro: You have a knack for connecting people. When things feel awkward, you naturally build the bridge — but in the middle of all that, your own position often disappears. This report follows the pattern underneath that: why you keep absorbing friction, what it costs you, and how to step out of the middle without losing love.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You have a knack for connecting people.

When two people feel awkward around each other, you naturally build a bridge. When someone's uncomfortable, you notice before they even say it. When a mood is about to break, you're the one who quietly steps in first and resets it.
So people say being around you, everyone ends up comfortable.
But here's the thing — you're fast at making everyone else comfortable. No one knows when you're uncomfortable.
Because you're the one who steps right in and smooths everything over, there's never any room left for your own position to even come up.

And there's one more thing — when someone's struggling, it somehow starts to feel like your responsibility. No one asked you to take it on. At some point, it just looks like a problem that's yours to solve.
So love starts the same way. At first, things between you and them, and between them and the people around you, all flow naturally — because you're managing both sides.
But in the middle of all that, you're not actually standing anywhere yourself.
chapters.profile_note: High Care, Low Pride — Adjusting for everyone, but never quite making a place for yourself

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: The early stage of a relationship is good.
You're managing both sides — them and you, them and the people around you — so everything connects naturally.

But as time goes on, something strange happens. You slowly become the middle of the relationship. You smooth over the friction between them and other people, and you don't even let them deal with conflict between you and them — you step in and resolve it first, yourself.
One day you look and realize — you've been absorbing every bit of friction in the relationship in advance.
You never asked for this role. It just happens naturally. When they're struggling, that problem somehow becomes your problem too.
chapters.highlight: You're not exhausted because there's too much conflict. You're exhausted because every conflict has always disappeared by passing through you.

chart.title: THE GRAVITY CURVE
chart.description: The flow where you keep absorbing the relationship's friction, while your own conflicts pile up with nowhere to go.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: It's true that you like harmony, but the real thing is that you can't stand an uncomfortable atmosphere. When conflict shows up, you're the one who can't hold back and steps in to resolve it. More often than it's affection for harmony, it's an inability to tolerate awkwardness.
items.truth_short: You like harmony, but often you're reacting to how unbearable awkwardness feels.

items.truth: It's not that you can't say no. It's that saying no would leave a visible gap between two people, and you're rushing to fill that gap before it even opens. You're good at seeing the gaps between other people. You just never apply that same eye to the gap between yourself and everyone else.
items.truth_short: You can say no — but you rush to fill the gap it might create.

items.belief: I always have to be the one keeping the balance in the middle.
items.truth: No. Sometimes you're allowed to stand on one side too. What you need isn't balance. It's a position of your own.
items.truth_short: You don't always need balance. Sometimes you need a position of your own.

items.truth: You are good at mediating, but that's not the whole story. When someone's struggling, it somehow feels like your responsibility. If you don't fix it, you can't settle. So you step in — not because you're good at it, but because not doing it makes you more uncomfortable than doing it does.
items.truth_short: You mediate well, but sometimes you step in because not fixing it feels worse.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're particularly drawn to people who can't tolerate conflict and push it onto you to resolve.
At first it feels like you're helping by sorting things out from the middle.
But the longer the relationship goes on, you become the shock absorber for every conflict in it.
And honestly, you're vulnerable to people who need help. Someone struggling. Someone who can't find direction. Someone who needs you nearby.
The problem is, even after you've fallen for them, you still can't put down the caretaker role.
chapters.highlight: Someone who finds you comfortable uses your mediation. Someone who loves you tries to work it out themselves, even without your stepping in.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to get even better at mediating. You're already plenty good at it.
What you need is to occasionally not mediate.
The friction between them and other people. The conflict between you and them.
Letting the two of you work it out on your own — that's also a form of love.
chapters.action_sentence: In the right relationship, you don't have to stand in the middle every time something gets uncomfortable.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to connect people. You already know how to keep the balance. You already know how to make everyone comfortable.
What you're going to learn next is how to watch without stepping in.
Even when friction shows up. Even when it gets awkward.
The skill of letting people work it out with their own strength, instead of stepping in first.
chapters.transition_note: This isn't about caring less. It's about letting other people build the strength to meet conflict without using you as the bridge every time.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.description: Someone who can't decide things on their own. They always ask you what to do. At first you assume it's because they trust you. But over time, they turn into someone who can't decide anything without you.

Someone who doesn't know their own feelings. They get confused about what they're even feeling. You have to read it for them. You're always the one who has to say first, "aren't you upset about this?"
Someone who disappears when problems show up. When something goes wrong, they quietly slip their share onto you. They say "I don't know what to do," when really, they're just not doing it.

Someone who keeps demanding emotional reassurance. "Are we okay?" "You're not mad, are you?" "Do you still like me?" — these questions come often. It's not that they can't decide things — it's that they constantly need confirmation that the relationship itself is safe. You become the one who fills that need, every time. But that anxiety doesn't go away just because you keep reassuring them.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: This one showed up pretty early. You just thought it was simply your role.

signal_strength.label: When conflict comes up, you're the one who steps in first to smooth it over
You're already managing even their other relationships — family, friends
Even conflicts between you and them get a solution from you before they ever try to work it out themselves
Almost no one ever asks first what's bothering you

chapters.core_signal: Every bit of friction in the relationship disappears by passing through you, as a given. It's so taken for granted that no one even realizes it's work for you.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: Conflict can happen without you stepping in first.
checklist.description: The relationship doesn't fall apart just because you let people work it out themselves.

checklist.title: Stating your own position first isn't strange.
checklist.description: The relationship is fine even when you stand on one side instead of standing in the middle.

checklist.title: The relationship doesn't break just because you don't mediate.
checklist.description: It's good to occasionally confirm that people can work things out even without you.

checklist.title: Your mediation isn't taken for granted.
checklist.description: This matters. You stepping into the middle should always be something to be thankful for — not invisible background noise.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You can't stand unresolved conflict. The moment someone feels awkward, you step in automatically. So — you keep standing in the middle, but the moment conflict arises between you and someone else, no one steps in for you. The type that doesn't work for you: someone who can't tolerate conflict and always pushes it onto you, with no conflict-resolving ability of their own. If you don't step in, it just gets left awkward.
sections.who_steadies_you: There's also someone who's a surprisingly good fit: someone who tries to work it out themselves, even without you stepping in. With someone like this, the relationship runs fine even when you're not always standing in the middle.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You'll be drawn again to someone who can't handle conflict and pushes it onto you. That can't be stopped. That's just who you are. The issue was never the attraction itself — it's what you do after.
sections.next_signs: Remember the signs from Chapter 8? They usually showed up months in. This time they'll show up faster, smaller. When a small disagreement comes up, they just wait for you to mediate. They never try to work it out directly. If you see this within the first few weeks, that's the start.
sections.try_this: Normally you'd hand them a solution. This time, do the opposite — just once, don't give one. Just listen. When they bring you a problem, don't give the answer — just sit and listen. Then watch. Someone who's trying to find their own answer will start sorting out their own thoughts as they talk. Someone who's offloading responsibility will keep demanding, "so what should I do?"

action_plan.summary: You already know how to connect people and smooth over conflict. What's left is learning when to let others meet the friction themselves.
action_items: This week, let one small awkward moment exist without immediately fixing it.
When someone brings you conflict, listen first and wait before offering a solution.
Say your own position once before trying to balance everyone else's.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to stand between people. This report covered why you keep ending up in that position — and how you relearn what it means to step out of it.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
